data.title: John Lewis warns staff over return to the office to keep up with rivals
data.description:
John Lewis has told staff they must be in the office more often as it races to keep up with rivals, which have ditched remote working.

In a memo to head office staff, the retailer said it expected them to be working “more in person than not”, either in the office or out with suppliers and customers.

John Lewis, which also owns Waitrose, said the changes would help accelerate its turnaround and achieve “better outcomes” after it posted a £21m loss last year, compared with a pre-tax profit of £97m a year earlier.

It said many of its competitors had already implemented a return-to-the-office policy.

In an internal memo, John Lewis said: “Many organisations have noticed improvements in collaboration and culture alongside improved business performance as a result of a hybrid model – one that allows for more time in person while still enjoying the benefits of working time at home too.”

It added that central office teams, which include HR and finance departments, should be “spending more of [their] working week collaborating face to face with [their] team and others [they] work alongside”. The company has previously taken a more flexible approach to office attendance.

The retailer said the expected change meant it was currently exploring options to “create more space” in its existing offices so staff could come in more regularly.

For example, it said unused office space at the Waitrose headquarters in Bracknell, Berkshire, could be fitted with more desks.

John Lewis told staff this did not constitute a change in policy, saying it remained committed to “hybrid working”.

In 2024, Boots ordered staff back to their desks five days a week, while Morrisons ended working from home for head office staff last summer.

Marks & Spencer describes itself as an “in-person business”, saying on its website that support teams are in the office three days a week. “We believe that skills are developed through collaboration and that spending time together makes us more creative and connected,” the retailer says.

John Lewis previously championed a “blended approach” and in 2023 halved the size of its central London offices. The move was driven by thousands of staff choosing to continue working from home after Covid restrictions were lifted.

However, more recently, there has been a shift in its attitude toward homeworking. Last year, staff in John Lewis’s commercial team were told they should work no more than two days a week from home to accommodate training and development requirements.

A spokesman for John Lewis said: “While some in our industry are returning to the office full time, our policy hasn’t changed, and we are committed to the flexibility that comes with a hybrid approach.

“To drive collaboration, faster decision-making and creativity, our goal is for central teams to be in the office, with suppliers and visiting shops more than they are at home, so we are working with them to make this happen.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Iran war oil shock risks food shortages, warns World Bank
data.description:
The Iran war risks causing food shortages as soaring fertiliser prices push more farmers to the brink, the World Bank has warned.

As many as 45 million people worldwide could face “acute food insecurity” this year if the conflict in the Middle East continues, the organisation said.

It follows a warning that farmers will struggle to keep up food production as they face a 31pc increase in fertiliser prices this year.

About half the world’s supply of urea, a nitrogen-based fertiliser, and almost a third of ammonia typically flow through the Strait of Hormuz.

The World Bank said price rises would be more severe for urea as shipments have ground to a halt since Iran and the US blocked the strait.

The price of urea jumped 50pc in March alone, the biggest monthly increase in a decade. They are expected to rise by 60pc this year.

The World Bank said: “Fertiliser affordability will fall to its worst level since 2022, eroding farmers’ incomes and threatening future crop yields.

“If the conflict proves more prolonged, these pressures on food supply and affordability could push up to 45 million more people into acute food insecurity this year,” the organisation said in its latest commodity report.

Farmers are also grappling with higher diesel and gas prices, making it more expensive to run their machinery. Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens by the end of May, energy prices will still surge 24pc this year to a four-year high, the World Bank said.

Indermit Gill, its chief economist, said: “The war is hitting the global economy in cumulative waves. First, through higher energy prices, then, higher food prices and finally, higher inflation, which will push up interest rates.”

The World Bank had previously forecast that the price of agricultural commodities to fall this year, because the industry began 2026 with a grain surplus. However, the Bank’s food price index is now forecast to increase by 2pc.

Beef prices will surge 11pc this year, the World Bank warned, while palm oil and soybean oil prices will jump by more than 8pc. It said it expected barley and wheat to rise by about 4pc.

If urea prices stay higher for longer, then farmers will cut back on using fertiliser. “These effects could have a long tail, materialising as reduced crop yields in future growing seasons,” the World Bank said.

Oil, gas and fertiliser prices have risen more quickly in the first month of the Iran war than they did in the first month of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The World Bank’s 2026 forecasts are based on disruption in the Strait of Hormuz starting to ease in May, with shipping traffic returning close to pre-war levels by October.

It said: “Commodity prices could rise even higher, if hostilities escalate or supply disruptions from the war last longer than projected.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves hands Rolls-Royce £600m to build mini-nukes in Britain
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has been handed a £600m loan from Rachel Reeves to help accelerate the development of Britain’s first mini-nuclear power plant.

The Chancellor’s National Wealth Fund confirmed details of the borrowing facility on Monday, which will be used to build three small modular reactors (SMRs) at Wylfa on the Welsh island of Anglesey.

The manufacturing giant was selected as the Government’s preferred partner for the project last year.

However, design work will now begin after Rolls-Royce struck an agreement with Great British Energy – Nuclear (GBEN), the quango set up to manage the mini-nuclear programme.

GBEN said the contract would unlock the next stage of the project, which aims to help commercialise nascent SMR technology.

It will provide Rolls-Royce with funds to complete detailed design work, including regulatory applications and planning approvals.

The £600m loan is in addition to £2.6bn that Ms Reeves allocated to the scheme during last year’s spending review.

Construction of the power plant itself is not expected to begin until the early 2030s, with the facility set to come online in the mid-2030s. But GBEN is understood to be carrying out ground preparation works now.

The scheme is eventually expected to create 3,000 local jobs in Wylfa, Anglesey, as well as another 5,000 supply chain jobs across the country.

Ms Reeves said: “This investment, along with vital financing from the National Wealth Fund, will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, added: “At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain’s energy security.

“We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, home-grown power for decades to come.”

GBEN said the contract with Rolls-Royce stipulates that the company must hit various “milestones”, although it did not immediately clarify what those were.

The three mini reactors are expected to have a combined capacity of about 1.4 gigawatts, enough to power three million homes for more than 60 years.

SMRs will be constructed from “modules” built in factories and then transported to sites for assembly.

The idea is meant to ensure the plants can be produced efficiently at scale and more quickly than larger nuclear projects.

In the longer run, it is hoped their lower price tags will also make them more attractive to private investors, who have long viewed bigger plants as too expensive and risky to back without government support.

Major technology companies, including Google, Amazon and Facebook-owner Meta, are investing in potential SMR technologies in the hope they could eventually be used to supply power-hungry data centres needed to develop AI.

Rolls-Royce is also building six SMRs for the Czech government.

Simon Bowen, the chairman of GBEN, said: “This is an immense moment for the UK nuclear programme, our organisation and the industry as a whole.

“Today represents a true recognition of the efforts made across GBEN, Rolls-Royce SMR and Government to get to this point, and I’m hugely proud of the team for reaching this critical milestone.”

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, added: “This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JCB threatens to quit Britain over inheritance tax raid
data.description:
The heir to JCB’s multibillion-pound manufacturing fortune has threatened to quit Britain in protest against Rachel Reeves’s inheritance tax raid.

Jo Bamford, son of Lord Bamford, the JCB chairman, warned the Staffordshire-based business could easily relocate to America to avoid paying 20pc tax on assets worth more than £2.5m.

“The family tax is a real problem,” Mr Bamford told City AM, warning that JCB “could quite easily become an American business”.

“I love being in Britain,” he said. “I love being here. I love our factories. But I would say to a political party of any stripe, there’s only so much you can ultimately do.”

Founded in 1945, JCB is one of Britain’s biggest family-owned manufacturing companies. It has 11 factories in the UK and employs more than 8,000 workers.

However, Mr Bamford said JCB could consider its future in the UK because Labour is “hunting down” family businesses through its inheritance tax raid.

He said: “You want us, as a family, to invest here in Britain.

“You know, we have businesses everywhere around the world. We have them in India and China and Brazil.”

Mr Bamford added: “I’m here because I’m British, and I’m here and I employ people in Britain because I like British people and I like being in my part of the community.”

The levy was first announced by the Chancellor in late 2024, removing family business owners’ ability to pass down assets through generations without having to pay inheritance tax.

As of April, full tax relief will only apply to the first £2.5m of a business’s assets upon the owner’s death, with everything above that threshold subject to a 20pc tax.

This threshold had initially been set at £1m, but was increased after a backlash from rural voters.

Many business owners are concerned that they will be forced to sell off assets or reduce investment to pay the tax.

This has prompted a string of high-profile entrepreneurs to consider leaving Britain as part of a broader wealth exodus, driven also by Ms Reeves’s decision to abolish non-dom status.

Some have already left since Labour came to power, including Alan Howard, a billionaire hedge fund manager, John Fredriksen, a shipping magnate, and Lakshmi Mittal, a steel tycoon.

Speaking to The Telegraph last year, Sir Rocco Forte, the hotelier, said the inheritance tax changes were “one of the main causes for the exodus”.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We’ve listened and raised the relief threshold to £2.5m to protect more small family businesses, while ensuring the largest make a fair contribution so we can deliver support for families and businesses, including cutting the cost of living.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Scandal-hit NS&I raises rates on savings accounts
data.description:
National Savings and Investments (NS&I) has increased its savings rates just weeks after it admitted to misplacing nearly £500m in people’s savings.

The Treasury-backed bank now offers some of the leading rates on the market, with its one-year fixed bond paying 4.5pc interest, up from 4.07pc.

The significant rate bumps come after The Telegraph revealed the provider lost track of investments, delaying payouts to bereaved families and withholding premium bond prizes.

The debacle resulted in the resignation of Dax Harkins, the bank’s long-serving former chief executive.

Before this morning, NS&I’s savings rates had been languishing midway down comparison tables. Its one-year fixed bond has increased from 4.07pc to 4.5pc, while the two-year bond pays 4.48pc and the three-year bond 4.45pc.

The new rates are available to new customers and savers with existing bonds that are due to mature.

Sarah Coles, of AJ Bell, said that NS&I was “clearly working harder to attract more cash” with a “fairly punchy” funding target of £15bn for this financial year.

“These are significant rate bumps, putting the fixed rate accounts within shouting distance of the front-runners,” she said.

“The one-year deal is particularly strong, and given that savers are far more likely to fix for one year than any other period, NS&I is clearly working harder to attract more cash.

She added: “It has clearly decided that it needs to do something bold to start the year strong.”

NS&I said the rate increases “reflect changes in the wider market” and will help meet its net financing target of £15bn this financial year.

The 4.5pc rate on the one-year guaranteed growth bonds is 0.16 percentage points shy of the market-leading offer from MBNA. The average one-year fix, based on a £10,000 deposit, currently stands at 4.04pc, according to analysts Moneyfacts.

The conflict in the Middle East has prompted expectations that interest rates could remain higher for longer, with the Bank of England’s next interest rate decision coming on Thursday.

As it stands, the best bond rate on the market is the five-year fixed term offered by Market Harborough Building Society, paying 4.7pc. NS&I’s equivalent product offers 4.4pc.

Rachel Springall, of Moneyfacts, said: “NS&I is popular because it is a trusted brand and provides 100pc capital security, so these bonds may appeal to savers with big pots who are happy to forgo higher interest rates available elsewhere.

“NS&I traditionally would react to any interest rate moves within the wider markets, to ensure they are not sitting head and shoulders above the competition. When the UK experiences an economic crisis, NS&I can step up to entice deposits to help the Government to raise money and support the economy, which we have seen in the past.”

NS&I is one of Britain’s biggest banks, looking after around £100bn for more than 26 million people. It is overseen by Rachel Reeves as an “executive agency” of the Chancellor.

A report into the payouts scandal is expected to be published in the coming weeks. It will reveal exactly how many customers have been affected, how their savings will be returned with interest and what additional compensation they may be eligible to receive.

The Treasury insisted it only became aware of the true scale of the failings in December, after being briefed on the issue by NS&I officials.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband brands BP’s profits ‘morally and economically wrong’
data.description:
Ed Miliband has branded BP’s profits “morally and economically wrong” after the war in Iran helped the oil major more than double its earnings.

The Energy Secretary launched an attack on the FTSE 100 giant as the company revealed profits hit $3.2bn (£2.4bn) between January and March, compared with $1.4bn in 2025. BP described the results as “exceptional”.

Mr Miliband condemned the company for making billions of pounds while households face higher energy bills because of rising oil and gas prices.

Posting on X, he wrote: “Profiting from a crisis is morally and economically wrong. That’s why we are taxing these windfall profits to help fund support with the cost of living. And why the Tories, Reform and SNP are utterly wrong to oppose the windfall tax.”

The Energy Secretary later deleted his post and replaced it with a toned-down version. In it, he said instead that it would be “completely wrong for a government to stand by and allow companies to make excess profits from a war”.

His comments triggered a backlash from oil experts.

BP’s surge in profits was driven largely by its oil trading business.

The company is one of the world’s largest oil traders, betting on price swings caused by geopolitical events such as tensions in the Middle East. BP often trades four billion barrels of oil a year – more than double what it actually produces.

Critics claimed this meant that Mr Miliband was wrong to suggest Labour is “taxing these windfall profits” .

The UK windfall tax only applies to oil and gas produced in UK waters, not to global oil trading. Profits made overseas are also taxed in the countries where they are recorded and cannot be taxed again in the UK.

Miliband’s ‘deliberately dishonest narrative’

Russell Borthwick, who runs the chamber of commerce in Aberdeen, accused Mr Miliband of dishonesty.

He said: “Energy companies pay their taxes, where due, on the profits they make. Ed Miliband knows this but he is peddling a deliberately dishonest narrative in relation to the North Sea.”

Mr Miliband’s attack is also likely to revive tensions with Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, who is expected to bank billions of pounds in additional taxes as a result of energy companies’ inflated oil and gas profits.

The row over BP’s profit comes ahead of an expected surge in earnings at all the global oil majors, fuelled by the conflict in the Middle East.

US and Israeli attacks on Iran have led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows.

The closure has caused huge swings in the price of Brent crude.

The oil price hit $112 on Tuesday, its highest level since Donald Trump announced a ceasefire with Iran earlier this month. At the start of the year, it was trading at less than $60 a barrel.

BP’s update on Tuesday marked its first set of results under Meg O’Neill, its new chief executive who started on April 1. She said the company was “running well”.

BP shares have been the sector’s worst-performing since 2020 after a botched net-zero investment drive.

However, the recent oil price jump has reversed that slide, with BP’s shares rising by around 24pc since the start of the war.

BP rose by more than 2pc in early trading on Tuesday, although it later dropped back down.

The company’s strength in oil trading comes from its control of actual oil movements. It manages around 300 chartered and owned tankers, allowing it to reroute cargoes around the world to wherever offers the best price.

Greg Newman, of Onyx Capital, a London-based trader in oil and oil derivatives, said Mr Miliband seemed to misunderstand how the industry worked.

“Oil trading desks exist precisely to manage risk and keep physical barrels moving when markets are dislocated. Without that function, price spikes are sharper, supply disruptions are worse and consumers pay more, not less.

“Risk-takers earn variable returns, that’s the entire basis of the activity.

“As someone who runs an oil trading business in London, I can tell you the last two years have been brutal for large parts of this industry – firms have folded, desks have been shut, books have lost serious money. No one was calling for a rebate then. You don’t get to claim the upside is immoral and ignore the downside that comes with it.”

However, environmental groups were quick to criticise the scale of BP’s profits, claiming they justified the windfall tax imposed on UK offshore operators including BP.

Simon Francis, of End Fuel Poverty, said: “These astronomical profits are a startling reminder that when conflict drives up the price of oil and gas, energy companies profit and households pay.

“We are now seeing the level of profits being posted, which shows why a windfall tax is so necessary.”

Overall profits attributable to shareholders in the first three months of the year soared to $3.8bn, up from $700m in the same period last year.

BP declined to comment on Mr Miliband’s remarks.

data.title: Britain faces food shortages because of Iran war
data.description:
Britain is facing a summer of food shortages because of the Iran war. A shortage of carbon dioxide could mean chicken, pork and fizzy drinks are in short supply if the conflict continues, secret government contingency planning for a “worst-case scenario” reveals.

Senior officials from departments including No 10, the Treasury, and the Ministry of Defence rehearsed scenarios examining the potential impact of the war on British industry in an event codenamed Exercise Turnstone.

The contingency plans, seen by The Times, were based on a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and no deal to end the war has been reached by mid-June. Farmers were warned of potential disruption and hospitality businesses, already squeezed by Labour’s tax rises, could be hit first by a shortage of CO2.

This is because CO2 is used in the process of slaughtering pigs and chickens and making drinks fizzy. It also improves the shelf life of foods such as salads, packaged meats and cakes. 

Officials also believe a collapse in CO2 supplies could endanger lives by making dry ice used to cool blood supplies, organs and vaccines scarcer. Under a reasonable worst-case scenario, CO2 supplies would fall to just 18 per cent of current levels, and factories would be ordered to stop other manufacturing to boost supplies by up to 100 per cent.

Emergency legislation to compel factories to co-operate has been discussed, with suppliers being compensated for stopping production of their main products.

The worst-case scenario involved an important UK plant suffering a mechanical error, while high gas costs led to lower production of ammonia and fertiliser, which produces CO2 as a by-product across Europe.

Critical food shortages are not expected, but shoppers could see fewer variety of goods on supermarket shelves.

Britain is expected to be hit hard by the economic fallout from the war.

On Wednesday, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) forecast that Britain is expected to suffer the biggest hit to growth of any G7 economy.

In a triple blow for Rachel Reeves, the IMF cut its UK growth forecasts for this year and next, warned of the highest inflation in the G7 and predicted one of the smallest increases in living standards in the world.

Higher fuel prices and energy bills are forecast to cost households £480 this year, according to the Resolution Foundation, with middle-class families being hit hardest.

On Thursday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for food shortages was under way and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course we don’t comment on leaks. But this now is out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and that he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

A government spokesman said: “We took decisive action last month to shore up the UK’s critical supplies of CO2 by temporarily restarting the Ensus bioethanol plant in Teesside and are continuing to work closely with business groups to tackle the impacts of events in the Middle East.

“Reasonable worst-case scenarios are a planning tool used by experts and are not a prediction of future events.”

data.title: How to buy and rent out property in Spain
data.description:
This is the first in a series of guides on buying and letting property abroad.

Are you the landlord of an overseas property? We’d like to hear about your experience – email money@telegraph.co.uk

Spain has long been a favourite for British holidaymakers. In 2025, 19.1 million of us flocked to the country to enjoy its great weather, beaches, culture and food.

It’s not surprising, then, that it’s also a popular choice for British expats – around 350,000 are registered as residents in the country – as well as those looking to buy and let holiday homes abroad.

If you’re considering an investment in a Spanish property, there’s a lot to consider first – not least the threat of increased taxes for buyers outside the EU.

Here, Telegraph Money asks mortgage, tax and legal experts to share their tips. This guide will cover:

  • How to find the best investments
  • What yields can I expect?
  • How easy is it to get a mortgage?
  • What are the tax implications?
  • What pitfalls should you be aware of?

How to find the best investments

Choosing a location for your property search in Spain will be dictated by your budget and what you want from your investment. If you plan to holiday there as well or keep it for retirement, it’s important to find a place you love. However, if it’s purely an investment, it comes down to what your ideal tenants will be looking for.

Alex Radford, partner at Spain-based law firm English Solicitor and Abogado, recommends what he calls “lock and goes”. These can be described as simple properties, such as two-bedroom and two-bathroom accommodations, which require minimal maintenance and are in easily accessible areas.

“You want somewhere within an hour of an airport, with good facilities, a swimming pool and access to the beach. You want to walk out and have everything on the doorstep.”

While there might be a temptation to invest in a pretty rural property, you might find it more difficult to find tenants for it. He added: “If you prefer a rural property and want to rent it out, you’ll need a good agent.”

In terms of areas to consider, he said: “The Costa Del Sol is still the hottest spot for property, but we’ve just opened up an office in Mojácar in Almeria.” The area, which has long been a popular spot with Spanish holidaymakers, can be more affordable than other parts of Spain and is becoming increasingly popular with British visitors.

In the Costa del Sol, Radford says you’ll generally need £400,000-£500,000 for a lock and go, but it’s possible to bag bargains starting from £100,000 in the Costa Blanca or Almeria.

What yields can I expect?

The average rental yield in Spain is currently 5.45pc, before tax, according to the Global Property Guide. However, while short-term lets will typically offer higher returns, it’s important to be aware that there will always be significant variations depending on where and what you purchase.

Peter Esders, legal director at Judicare, said: “Traditionally, you would be looking at something like 5-8pc yields, but these will very much depend on not only the region and area but also the property. If you buy the wrong property, then even being in a good area won’t help. Equally, you can buy the best property in the wrong area and won’t be able to rent it out.”

For this reason, it’s a good idea to seek the help of agents with a good knowledge of the area you’re looking to buy in – or to make sure you know the area well yourself.

In addition to a steady income, there is also potential for capital growth, with house prices continuing to rise across Spain. According to figures from Global Property Guide, house prices across Spain were up by 12.89pc in the final quarter of 2025, compared to the same period in the previous year.

However, while the Spanish market might look healthy, there are headwinds for UK investors.

Amid concerns that local buyers cannot afford property in many parts of Spain, the government proposed a new 100pc tax on purchases from buyers outside of the EU last year. This hasn’t yet come to pass – and some say it never will – but locals’ displeasure with foreign buyers hasn’t gone away. 

Earlier this year there were reports the Balearic Islands government was considering banning British buyers from purchasing property in Mallorca, Menorca and Ibiza.

There are plenty of existing rules to abide by. Introduced last year, those entering the short-term rental market must register rental properties with the authorities and get a reference number – you won’t be able to list on booking websites like Airbnb without one.

If you’re buying an apartment in a block, you’ll also need the approval of 60pc of the community of owners to get a rental licence. This is part of moves to give local property owners a say in whether or not they’re happy having holidaymakers as neighbours.

“Some won’t allow it,” said Mr Radford. “Or they might charge more in community fees.”

Complicating matters further is the fact that different regions and authorities within Spain are also able to impose their own rules on the issuing of licences. Seville, for example, has introduced a cap on licences, while Malaga has frozen short-term licences in some districts for three years.

As such, Mr Radford suggests that if you’re looking for an apartment or townhouse in a block, ask your agent to only show you properties that can be rented out.

Before you choose a property, it’s also important to factor in the full cost of buying it, with taxes varying between regions. Mr Radford suggested budgeting for up to 14pc of the purchase price to cover costs, including transfer tax and legal fees.

How easy is it to get a mortgage?

Getting a mortgage overseas isn’t always easy. However, Miranda John, director of international property at mortgage broker SPF Private Clients, said: “The high street Spanish lenders are pretty flexible on where a potential borrower is resident. Spanish mortgages are always on a repayment basis, and LTVs [loan-to-values] are a maximum of 70pc unless you are a Spanish resident.

“For property of €2m or more, this usually falls to 60pc. The pricing is competitive, and there are attractive short and long-term fixed rates.”

She added that if you’re looking to buy a rural property, you might find you need to stump up a bigger deposit, with maximum LTVs potentially limited to 50pc.

Another option is to remortgage your UK property and use the money raised to buy the property outright. Louis Levine, senior mortgage broker at Orton Financial, said: “UK high street lenders are generally comfortable with this, provided you can clearly evidence how the funds will be used. For larger purchases (above €1m), specialist borrowing options are available too with greater flexibility.”

If you’re considering using a mortgage to buy a property in Spain, it’s worth consulting a broker who specialises in overseas mortgages.

Simon Conn, an overseas mortgage broker, recommends getting a mortgage in principle before agreeing to buy the property and having an opt-out clause in case the loan falls through.

“Arrange your mortgage in the currency that you earn in when possible, but if you receive rental income in local currency, this can possibly be used to offset the monthly repayments,” he added.

What are the tax implications?

If you’re a resident of the UK but let out a property in Spain, you’ll need to pay tax on your rental income in Spain, but also declare it in the UK as part of your worldwide income.

However, you won’t pay tax twice. “Because we have a double taxation treaty, you only pay any difference between the two in the UK,” Mr Esders explained.

“There is a temptation for some people to be very selective with where they declare the income. They don’t declare the income in Spain if they rent out to other British people and get paid in Sterling, and then persuade themselves that they do not have to declare the income in the UK on the basis that the property is in Spain. Neither of those things [are] correct.”

When the property is sold, there will also be tax to pay on capital gains. “You primarily pay that in Spain on any gain that you have made, and then you also declare that in the UK, but again the double taxation treaty means that you only pay any difference in the UK,” Mr Esders said.

What pitfalls should you be aware of?

The key to making a successful investment is to get the right advice. The biggest pitfall, according to Mr Radford, is rushing into a purchase. “Agents will talk about location, location, location – but we say it’s preparation, preparation, preparation.

“For example, you might want to talk to your lawyer about ownership options to ensure your purchase is as tax-effective as possible.”

Mr Conn also stressed the importance of having an independent building survey – even if you’re looking to buy a new-build – and that you understand what you’re agreeing to. “Check the translation of what you are signing, and if it’s an ‘English version’, ensure it is a true translation of the original.”

While some buyers might just want their property to “wash its face” while they aren’t using it, if you’re relying on its investment potential, you also need to be realistic about the money it will make you.

“It is vital to go through the figures and be realistic about how much you can rent it out for. A seller may tell you that you can rent it out for €x a week – but for how many weeks a year?” said Mr Esders. “They may tell you that you can rent it out X number of weeks a year, but at what price? Generally, you will rent out less than you think you will and will make less than you think.”

Finally, be careful of where your advice comes from. As Mr Esders said: “One of the issues with Spain is that there are many people who think that they know what the legal situation is and are happy to dish out that advice on the internet but who don’t really understand and therefore end up giving bad advice.”

You might also find there’s a difference between what the law says and what people actually do, he added. “That is why it is vital to get independent professional advice from specialists who understand both Spanish and UK law and tax. We spend a lot of our time sorting out problems for people who didn’t take legal or tax advice in the first place or took bad advice from the internet.”

data.title: Britain has record taxes yet Labour’s mismanagement has left us broke
data.description:
The Governor of the Bank of England has warned that the world is facing a “very big energy shock” that will push up prices.

A statement of the bleeding obvious, as Basil Fawlty might say? Perhaps, but it serves to highlight just how insufficient the response has been to the economic fallout from Donald Trump’s misadventures in the Middle East.

The Government is so far out of its depth that armbands and a rubber ring may have to become compulsory items at future Cabinet meetings.

The latest iteration of the energy support scheme for industry is merely further evidence that ministers either still don’t get it or aren’t willing to provide meaningful support.

The decision to rethink the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which provides a 25pc cut in bills for so-called energy-intensive users, was supposed to demonstrate that someone in Government finally had grasped the severity of the situation facing manufacturers.

Instead, the opposite is true. Sure, expanding the breadth of it to include over 10,000 factories rather than the 7,000 first proposed is meaningful, but not if support isn’t brought forward. For reasons no one in power has been able to provide a compelling explanation for, those that qualify for relief still won’t be able to access it until April 2027.

The mind truly boggles. Without real help now, Britain risks losing some of the most critical parts of the supply chain forever, undermining our already fragile national sovereignty further and making us even more reliant on volatile foreign suppliers for materials that underpin scores of vital products.

The American owner of the UK’s last producer of a critical chemical called aniline has warned that it would close the site in Wilton, Teesside, if energy prices remained at their current levels for the next three months.

It means the amended BICS scheme, which Peter Kyle wasted little time in championing on national radio on Thursday, won’t make the slightest jot of difference to the fate of at least one major factory. The future of many more will be hanging in the balance.

The Business Secretary had the audacity to claim the changes to BICS showed Labour was “a government of action” that would “always do what’s needed” in times of global instability. Twaddle.

The only sensible conclusion is that Labour’s woeful mismanagement of the economy, under which welfare is being repeatedly prioritised over everything else including even our national security, has rendered Britain so broke that it could not afford to respond in times of genuine crisis.

This, of course, shouldn’t be the case. As the International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out this week, Rachel Reeves has raised taxes at the fastest pace in the world.

The Chancellor’s repeated raids on households and businesses would push the UK’s tax burden to a peacetime high of 42pc of GDP by the start of the next decade, the IMF’s figures showed. This equates to an extra £130bn of taxes since Labour took power, or a staggering £4,500 more per household.

But because of this Government’s obsession with welfare and the NHS, there is no money for energy or defence – or much else, for that matter. As Anna Leach, the chief economist at the Institute of Directors, points out, the UK is “uniquely vulnerable” to events in the Middle East because of “financing conditions, high initial starting point for inflation, uncompetitive energy costs and low fiscal space”.

It explains why the IMF expects growth in Britain this year to be slower, and inflation higher than any other major advanced economy.

The problem is spending, or rather where that spending is being directed. The intervention of Lord Robertson – a Labour grandee, former secretary-general of Nato, as well as the author of the Government’s recent strategic defence review – is telling.

Perhaps it will prove to be decisive in ensuring that some of the ever-expanding welfare budget is redirected to other departments. When serious figures like Robertson are starting to openly question the benefits bill, it is surely time for a complete rethink of where resources are being channelled. As he points out, Britain’s welfare budget is now five times the amount we spend on defence at a time when national security has never been more vital.

Perhaps naive ministers have allowed the latest GDP figures to fool them into thinking the economy could weather the incoming storm.

No doubt February’s numbers showing a 0.5pc rise in GDP are a pleasant surprise, but any growth had almost certainly already been extinguished by events in the Middle East – Kyle himself acknowledged this.

It is why the failure to provide meaningful and immediate support for heavy industry makes no sense. Left to fend for themselves in the face of even higher energy and raw material costs than they were already grappling with thanks to the war in Ukraine, and endless government-imposed green levies, manufacturers will be forced to curtail investment, cut back on hiring, and possibly even reduce output. Under those circumstances, growth becomes little more than an aspiration.

Yet, at the same time, ministers are drawing up plans to invoke emergency legislation to force many of those very same factories to double CO2 production to avoid shortages that will ripple through the economy. Forget a fizzy drinks crisis, a CO2 scarcity could quickly become a threat to life by affecting supplies of vaccines, blood and vital medicines.

Given this, you would think ministers would be bending over backwards to provide help. Instead, the best they could come up with was a rehashed scheme that still wouldn’t be available for another year – proof that the disconnect at the top has reached epic new proportions.

On Thursday, Sir Keir Starmer told tech bosses hauled into No 10 to discuss child safety: “The status quo simply cannot be allowed to stand.” With local elections around the corner, the same could be said of Labour’s time in power.

data.title: British potteries are in trouble. Here are the everyday ceramics to buy and use
data.description:
News of Denby Pottery calling in the administrators has brought the challenges faced by the British ceramics industry into sharp focus. Rising costs surrounding employment and energy have left the 217-year-old business, which produces all of its collections from its site in Derbyshire, operating at a loss. It is looking for a new buyer and has reportedly cut its 500-plus workforce by 80.

Denby isn’t the only one feeling the pinch. Mike Raybould, the chief executive of Portmeirion Group, says: “Energy remains one of the most significant pressures on our industry. Ceramics production is inherently energy-intensive, so sustained increases in energy prices have a direct impact on the cost of manufacturing in the UK.” Portmeirion, which was founded in the late 1960s, produces 100,000 kiln-fired pieces a week from its factory in Stoke-on-Trent and is stocked in around 60 countries. “The ‘made in the UK’ backstamp carries a huge amount of value in the global market,” adds Raybould.

Public support for Denby has been mobilised with a #SaveDenby campaign, but it will take more than just a hashtag to save the business. Raybould notes that the ceramics sector has called on the Government to be eligible for energy support schemes such as the British Industry Supercharger, which benefits steel, metal and chemical producers.

Then there is the issue around skills and recruitment. Emma Bridgewater, which also produces its ranges in The Potteries, has an ageing workforce. “So many of our highly skilled makers are coming up for retirement. The industry hasn’t had an entry programme or apprenticeship programme since the 1980s,” says a spokesperson. That will change next month, when six new recruits join the inaugural Craft Apprenticeship Scheme, which covers every element of the ceramic manufacturing process.

Emma Bridgewater, which was founded in 1986, has amassed a 100,000-strong base of ardent fans. “All of our pottery is hand-signed, and some customers will collect a particular decorator’s work and come to meet them at the factory, so there’s a real connection,” adds the spokesman. “By investing in buying British pottery to eat your breakfast off, you’re supporting an industry-wide regeneration of skills and expertise in giving young people in a city an opportunity to develop a career. It really reaches deep.”

On that note, here are some of the best buys to elevate the everyday in the kitchen, all proudly made in the UK.

data.title: Where to invest in buy-to-let with £50k
data.description:
As Britain’s landlords prepare for the burdens of the Renters’ Rights Act, many are leaving the sector – or at least holding back on expanding their buy-to-let portfolios.

But it’s not all doom and gloom. It’s still possible to earn yields of up to 12.5pc in the UK, and you only need an investment of £50,000 to find them.

Using data from Hamptons Estate Agents, we can reveal the highest yields available across flats, terraced properties and semi-detached houses for those with £50,000 in cash to spend, covering a 25pc deposit and stamp duty.

While it’s possible to find high yields with just £25,000, having a larger budget to play with means you have a greater choice of prospective properties, but you’ll still need to look beyond the south of England.

We’ve highlighted three areas below, offering the top average yields and expert insight into what the rental market there has to offer.

Where will £50,000 earn you the highest rental returns?

The table below shows the areas with the highest buy-to-let returns for a £50,000 budget across flats, terraced houses and semi-detached properties, in order of average yields on flats.

Blaenau Gwent

A £50,000 deposit would go a long way in the former Welsh mining stronghold of Blaenau Gwent, which borders Monmouthshire and Torfaen.

While a landlord can earn a yield of 11.2pc with just a £25,000 budget, a £50,000 budget could give your rental income a boost and provide double-digit yields from a terraced or semi-detached house.

A landlord would need a £31,309 deposit to put 25pc towards an average terraced property priced at £125,237 in the area.

Stamp duty costs of £6,267 push the total upfront to £37,576, providing plenty of spare change from the £50,000.

The average monthly rent for a terraced property is £1,106 in Blaenau Gwent, providing an annual gross yield of 10.6pc.

A £50,000 budget could stretch to a semi-detached house to rent out in the area. The average price for this is currently £150,802, so a 25pc deposit would require £37,701. Stamp duty would add £8,056 to the up-front cost, but you’ll still come in under £50,000 at £45,757.

The typical rent for a semi-detached house is £1,256 per month, providing an annual gross yield of 10pc.

The average gross yield across all property types that can be accessed with a £50,000 deposit in Blaenau Gwent – flats, terraced and semi-detached – is 10.6pc, according to Hamptons.

Angela Davey, of Welsh estate agency Peter Alan, said landlords were attracted by the high yields and reliable tenant type, mainly drawn from locals.

The area is only about an hour from Cardiff, and Ms Davey said this attracts foreign students who often pay higher rents.

She said: “There is also a massive market of young couples and families who can’t buy, but rent for a few years while they save for a deposit.

“In some cases, they end up buying the house from the landlord.

“Just as the lack of supply is keeping rents high nationally, it is pushing them up in this little valley.”

Blackburn with Darwen

Blackburn with Darwen, Lancashire, a former cotton textile powerhouse, can provide high yields for landlords with just a £50,000 deposit.

With a 25pc mortgage deposit, a landlord could get a flat or even stretch to a terraced property to rent out in the area.

The average price for a flat in Blackburn with Darwen costs £88,683, according to Hamptons.

A landlord would need £22,171 up front for a 25pc deposit, plus £4,434 for stamp duty, for a total of £26,605, leaving money leftover from the £50,000 goal.

The average rent on a flat in Blackburn with Darwen is £832 per month, delivering an annual gross yield of 11.3pc.

Alternatively, a typical terraced home in the area costs £131,999, according to Hamptons.

This would require a 25pc deposit of £33,000 towards the buy-to-let mortgage and £6,740 in stamp duty, giving a total upfront cost of £39,740.

A typical rent of £1,049 per month would yield a gross 9.5pc for a landlord.

Both property types provide an average gross yield of 10.4pc.

Hyndburn

Hyndburn, located in Lancashire, was once well known for its textile and coal industries, and the area even produced materials used to build the Blackpool Tower and the Empire State Building.

Landlords can also achieve higher returns in the area with a £50,000 deposit.

While £25,000 would be enough to generate a yield of up to 12.5pc from a flat, increasing the budget to £50,000 could secure a typical terraced or semi-detached property in the area.

The average price of a terraced property in Hyndburn, according to Hamptons, is £113,085. A landlord would need £28,271 for a 25pc deposit, plus £5,654 for stamp duty – a total of £33,925.

The average rent on a terraced house in Hyndburn is £934 per month, providing a gross yield of 9.9pc.

Similarly, a semi-detached home in Hyndburn costs an average of £153,510. A landlord would need £38,378 for a 25pc deposit, and £8,246 for stamp duty. You would still be in the £50,000 bracket with a total cost of £46,624. Average monthly rents of £1,051 would then generate a decent 8.2pc gross yield.

The average gross yield across all property types in Hyndburn, based on a £50,000 budget, is 10.2pc.

Matt Willcock, of Mortimers Estate Agents in Accrington, said: “The area is well connected by rail and road to nearby towns and cities like Manchester, Blackburn, Burnley and Bury, which is a pull for renters, who can commute easily while enjoying more affordable rents.

“There are also some great eateries in the area, and the Market Hall in Accrington is currently undergoing a multi-million-pound refurbishment to transform it into a food and drink hall. This will help transform the area and make it even more popular amongst renters, which will pull in more landlords.”

He said neighbouring areas such as Blackburn with Darwen and Rossendale have seen substantial house price growth over the past few years, so Hyndburn is now likely to follow suit, which is attractive to landlords looking to buy.

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine lives’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: Rejoice! The English sports car is coming back
data.description:
Join me, if you will, for a stroll through time to the garage of memories at the end of the garden, where the pungent odour of motor oil mixes with mouldering grass cuttings.

In here you might find your father’s MGB roadster from 1968 – the one in Old English White with chrome bumpers, wire-spoke wheels and the black leather interior that reeked of Rothmans.

The canvas roof always leaked, but it was a joy to be in – even if its restrained good looks and rather limited power made it the sports car equivalent of a Rich Tea Biscuit.

Perhaps, however, the English sports car of yesteryear in your garage is the peerless Austin Healey 3000 from the 1960s – an aesthetic masterpiece, though not if it’s been rolled through a hedge (which is what my father did to his mother’s Healey after a binge at the George and Dragon in Epping).

Or how about the angular 1970s Triumph TR7? My Uncle William had one in burnt gold – which my cousin Andy used to rearrange a lamppost. William had it rebuilt. But you would.

Whichever old English sports car inhabits your own showroom of nostalgia, whether it’s one of the above or a grunty TVR Griffith or a lithe Lotus Esprit – like Roger Moore had in For Yours Eyes Only, or Julia Roberts floored in Pretty Woman – it’s a sad fact that while a few are still on the roads today, the companies that made them have either vanished or changed beyond recognition.

MG was mauled badly before being sold to the Chinese – who moved production to China in 2016. Triumph disappeared into the catastrophe of the demise of British Leyland. Lotus is owned by Geely, another Chinese giant, which announced it was cutting a third of the workforce – 550 jobs – at its HQ and factory in Norfolk last year. TVR exists and there is news of a new model… again.

Last time I heard Morgan, the venerable maker of wooden-framed sports cars from Malvern was thriving, albeit in the hands of a Continental industrial conglomerate, and then there’s Jaguar, which had the F-Type until it ceased production in 2024, though it was hardly the “spiritual successor” of the E-Type – a serious contender for the title of most beautiful sports car of all time. Aston Martin, the modern Bond’s wheels of choice, trundles on but is seemingly mired in commercial woes.

So it’s heartening that in these dark days, where good news is about as rare as a Resident Doctor on duty in England, that lovers of English sports cars have something to celebrate.

Because a new sports car start-up – Longbow – has unveiled a spankingly beautiful all-electric open-top “Speedster” model, which will do 0-60 in 3.5 seconds and allegedly go 275 miles on battery power. While the £84,995-Speedster is a rich-man’s toy – there’s no roof or windscreen – and is available only in a limited 150-model run (most of which are already sold), next year Longbow plans to begin making a Roadster model – a two-seater with a roof that will be priced at £64,000 and do 280 miles.

What’s more, they hope to make 5,000 of them a year with an entirely British supply chain, except for batteries which aren’t yet made here – but when they are, they will be.

If it all transpires, this will create and secure several thousand British jobs in the automotive sector. And it’s just the start: the firm would like to expand into bigger cars.

While I don’t think they have received a single handout from the Government, they do stand to benefit from Rachel Reeves’s economic policies because there are now quite a lot of unemployed automotive workers out there looking for new jobs.

England, says Longbow’s co-founder Mark Tapscott, who is ex-Tesla and BYD, is still the best place in the world to make a light-weight sports car, and what could be better if you’re embracing an electric future where every gram counts? Astonishingly, the Roadster weighs just 995 kilos, so about half of a Tesla Y or, funnily enough, about the same as my dad’s little old MGB.

Which is technologically impressive, and should give us all hope. Because while 5,000 cars a year – assuming they achieve it – is small beer compared with giants like Tesla, it’s a step in the right direction – and one that builds on a great English tradition of lightweight sports cars.

Not just that, it proves that despite the gloom, despite the burden of Labour’s taxes, regulation and energy costs, we can still do it. And, thank Heaven for that, because there’s no better way to explore the English countryside than behind the wheel of an English sports car. It’s what the Sussex Downs, the rolling hills of Hampshire and the Lake District were invented for.

data.title: Netflix co-founder to step down after spearheading streaming revolution
data.description:
The businessman who turned Netflix from a DVD delivery service into the world’s biggest streaming giant has announced plans to step down as chairman.

Reed Hastings, who co-founded Netflix alongside Marc Randolph in 1997, said on Thursday that he would leave the US media company after three decades in June.

It brings an end to 29 years at Netflix for Mr Hastings, who revolutionised TV entertainment and turned the US company into a $455bn media behemoth.

He is most credited with being the driving force behind Netflix’s pivot from delivering DVDs in red envelopes to becoming a worldwide streamer with original content.

Announcing his departure alongside Netflix’s latest results, which recorded revenue growth of 16pc to $12.3bn, Mr Hastings said: “Netflix changed my life in so many ways, and my all‑time favourite memory was January 2016, when we enabled nearly the entire planet to enjoy our service.”

Shares in Netflix fell by 9.3pc in the wake of the announcement.

Long-time Democrat donor

The billionaire businessman said he now planned to focus on “other pursuits”, potentially including the Utah ski resort that he bought in 2023.

He previously announced plans to turn half of the mountain into a members-only ski club, with accompanying residential developments.

Mr Hastings, who has previously served on the boards of Microsoft and Facebook, is also a long-time Democrat donor. He has given more than $20m to Democratic causes in recent years, including to Joe Biden’s 2020 and 2024 presidential campaigns.

However, in 2024, he told the New York Times that Mr Biden should withdraw from the race and “allow a vigorous democratic leader” to stand against Donald Trump.

Though generally viewed as a Silicon Valley liberal, he has more recently voiced support for Mr Trump’s $100,000 visa fee for foreign workers, calling it “a great solution”.

During his 24-year stint as chief executive between 1999 and 2023, Mr Hastings transformed Netflix into the most valuable entertainment company in the world, with an estimated audience approaching one billion.

He had previously boasted that he considered sleep to be the streaming platform’s biggest rival, telling an audience in Los Angeles in 2017: “We’re competing with sleep, on the margin. And so it’s a very large pool of time.”

Mr Hastings has been gradually winding down his responsibilities at Netflix over recent years. He appointed Ted Sarandos as his co-chief executive in 2020 before choosing Greg Peters to replace him in the joint leadership role in 2023.

Boosted by US price rises

His departure was announced alongside Netflix’s first set of results since the streaming giant backed out of a blockbuster bidding war to buy Warner Bros.

Netflix had struck an $83bn deal to buy the Hollywood studio, which is home to the Harry Potter film series and HBO TV shows, but was usurped when rival bidder Paramount tabled a knockout $111bn offer.

Netflix bosses have argued that they took a disciplined approach to the acquisition, insisting that Warner Bros was a “nice to have” at the right price.

Shares in the streaming service had risen almost 30pc since it backed out of the deal, which had not been viewed positively by investors. The company has also cashed in a $2.8bn break fee from Paramount, giving it extra firepower to fund new shows.

Netflix has also been boosted by recent price rises in the US – the second time it has increased prices in the last year.

The company, which no longer discloses subscriber numbers, has been building its cheaper, advertising-supported tier in an effort to hold on to viewers in the face of growing cost-of-living pressures.

It has also been pushing further into live sports as the streaming service seeks to generate more revenue from ads and keep audiences on its platform.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
