data.title: ‘You’re destroying the economy’: how Labour killed Britain’s thriving chemical industry
data.description:
Tim Carter slows the car to a crawl, eventually pulling over to allow for the full scale of what lies out of the window to be taken in. The sprawling Wilton petrochemicals site where he works, south of the River Tees, once thrummed with activity.

Opened in 1949 by chemicals powerhouse ICI, it quickly became one of Britain’s most important technical hubs, helping to propel the North East to the vanguard of advanced manufacturing.

Yet on a warm Tuesday morning in April, the 2,000-acre complex has the feel of a graveyard.

Framed by a bright blue sky, the skeletons of once-mighty industrial giants are dotted across the horizon in every direction – fossils from an era when Britain could still rely on such production strongholds to provide the building blocks that power a civilised, modern economy.

The plant that Carter oversees as operations site manager is at risk of being added to the casualty list, further degrading Britain’s capacity to produce what industrialists call “foundational chemicals”.

These are the substances that are essential in the production of plastics, fertilisers, pharmaceuticals, industrial materials, most consumer goods and scores of other products that most of us take for granted.

“The team are trying to remain focused on running the site as well as possible, because that’s the best thing that we can do to demonstrate the efficiency, the safety and also the long-term viability,” he says, careful not to stray from the official party line.

Still, such optimism may be in vain. Owned by America’s Huntsman Corporation, Peter Huntsman, its chairman and chief executive, has warned that the company may be forced to pull the plug on what is one of its last remaining UK outposts.

The chemicals industry says Britain is ignoring the struggles of specialist factories such as Huntsman’s at its peril. With a workforce of just 80 people, its fate may seem relatively inconsequential, but the plant produces aniline, a chemical used in the production of car seats, mattresses, footwear, drugs such as paracetamol and many other everyday products.

It is also the only one of its kind left standing in the whole of the UK.

Chemicals underpin 95pc of all manufactured goods. Yet, many other critical chemical facilities similar to Huntsman’s have vanished in recent years – victims of what the Ineos billionaire Sir Jim Ratcliffe has called “the deindustrialisation of the UK”.

However, despite its significance, bosses fear the hollowing out of the UK’s industrial base is in danger of almost being missed in the corridors of Westminster.

India’s Tata Chemicals pulled down the shutters permanently on its soda ash plant in Lostock, on the outskirts of Northwich, Cheshire, last year.

The decision not only brought to an end nearly 150 years of uninterrupted manufacturing on the site, but left Britain without its own supplies of sodium carbonate, a key ingredient in the manufacture of glass, paper, detergents, and to treat fresh water.

Tata’s plans were announced in 2024 but they didn’t appear in the national press until it was picked up by Sky News earlier this year.

Grant Pearson, chairman of Ensus, one of Britain’s last bioethanol producers, warns against complacency.

“If you don’t have a chemicals industry ... then you can’t have pharmaceutical production, aerospace production or car production,” he says.

“Without a core basic chemical manufacturing base, you’d end up with those businesses either moving overseas, or these industries totally reliant on overseas supply chains.”

The conflict in Iran has once again shone a spotlight on Britain’s vulnerability to global economic shocks from an acute over-reliance on foreign markets.

But could events in the Middle East prove to be a silver lining for Britain’s industrial future? Although the energy crunch threatens to unleash a new cost of living crisis, there are tentative hopes it could also act as a long overdue wake-up call for complacent policymakers.

Huntsman believes ministers have given “zero thought” to what the loss of its Wilton factory would mean for the economy. Yet the consequences of losing a vital link in a supply chain have the potential to be devastating, he says.

He points to Ineos’s salt plant in Runcorn, Cheshire, which the firm has threatened to close. “Wait until you have to be reliant on foreign powers to get the chemistry to clean your drinking water, or to treat your food, just because of one product out of one factory. That’s what you’re down to [in the UK],” he says.

Its uncertain future sums up the severity of Britain’s industrial decline. The factory is the last of its kind in the country. If it vanishes, Britain will become a net importer of salt for the first time in hundreds of years – a humiliating new low for a nation once known as “the workshop of the world”.

‘Existential’ energy costs

On the southern boundary road of the Wilton site, a mile and half from Huntsman’s aniline plant, is one of the UK’s last remaining bioethanol facilities.

For months, the Ensus factory sat idle, having been mothballed last September after a trade deal with Washington opened the floodgates to cheap imports from the US.

But just weeks after American bombs began raining down on Tehran, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, suddenly approved a £100m plan to reopen the complex.

The Government was propelled into action over fears that Britain was facing devastating shortages of CO2 – a byproduct of bioethanol production – vital in the manufacture of fizzy drinks, food packaging, IVF treatment and the stunning of animals in abattoirs.

It was the first major ministerial intervention to prevent shortages caused by the Iran war. Robin Brooks, senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, a US think tank, said manufacturing data published last week by S&P Global laid bare the scale of the UK’s exposure compared with other countries around the world.

“High oil prices and supply chain disruptions are taking their toll,” he says. “They’re hitting small, open economies like the UK and Japan hardest, while larger economies like the US and eurozone have so far seen modest hits.”

Pearson, the chairman of Ensus, hailed the Government’s intervention as proof that the UK needs to protect its own production of vital chemicals, rather than becoming even more reliant on imports.

“Right across the chemical sector in particular, we’re seeing closures of plants, which not only impact the jobs in those sectors, but means we’re not making those basic raw materials,” he said after ministers stepped in.

Kyle, meanwhile, said the Government had “acted swiftly to boost the resilience of our supply chains and protect critical UK sectors”.

Yet for all the triumphalism, UK chemical manufacturers insist the crisis they are grappling with is one very much made at home, thanks to the highest energy costs in the developed world.

Last year, Sir Jim’s Ineos announced the permanent closure of its ethanol plant in Grangemouth, Falkirk, with the loss of 80 jobs. The facility was one of only two plants in Europe producing synthetic ethanol, which is used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, plastics and paints.

Ineos blamed a doubling of UK energy prices over the last five years, “incentivising imports from the US” where energy prices were five times lower at the time.

According to the Office for National Statistics, British industry is at the greatest disadvantage to foreign competitors when it comes to electricity bills because the price of natural gas determines the cost of electricity 98pc of the time. Business leaders have repeatedly called for this anomaly to be addressed.

Although gas prices have come down from their post-Ukraine peak, they remain considerably higher than they were before Russia’s invasion. Britain’s vulnerability is made more acute by a reliance on overseas markets for as much as 60pc of its natural gas, and a growing reliance on more expensive liquefied natural gas from the US, Qatar and Africa.

UK companies also face comparatively higher costs for distribution and the upkeep of the pylons, cables and substations that make up the network.

With energy representing more than 50pc of many manufacturers’ cost base, such disparities can quickly become “existential”, says Steve Elliott, the chief executive of the Chemicals Industry Association (CIA).

Before the crisis, Huntsman Corporation’s energy costs in Britain were three to four times what they were elsewhere. The Fortune 500 company has operations in more than 25 countries across the globe.

Once American bombs started landing on Iran, the disparity rocketed to around nine times as natural gas prices went through the roof. The chasm leaves the future of its Wilton unit hanging in the balance, Huntsman said in March.

Punishing energy bills are being compounded by Ed Miliband’s obsession with reaching arbitrary net zero targets. Companies have been left contending with a mountain of government-imposed green levies and taxes including the climate change levy, renewables obligation and feed-in tariffs.

Elliott has dubbed it “decarbonisation through deindustrialisation,” and says it is no coincidence that a near 40pc fall in UK chemical production between between 2021 and 2024 was accompanied by a similar percentage reduction in the sector’s carbon emissions.

In February, the Chemicals Industry Association issued a fresh plea for “business-supportive policy”. Elliott said the collapse in output was the result of “successive government failures to support UK manufacturing”. Policy has been tantamount to “economic vandalism”, Elliott said.

Sir Jim suggested in December that carbon taxes are fundamentally flawed because “deindustrialising Britain achieves nothing for the environment. It merely shifts production and emissions elsewhere”.

There’s an irony, too, because net zero can’t be achieved without many of the chemicals that the UK is producing much less of in the face of uncompetitive energy costs, industry figures point out.

The production of electric vehicle batteries, wind turbines, solar panels and carbon capture technology are all heavily reliant on many building-block chemicals such as ethylene, propylene, ammonia and methanol that Britain either no longer produces or where home-grown supplies are considered scarce.

The same can be said of many of the industries that the Government has declared a priority in its recently updated industrial strategy.

Domino effect

A tour around the Wilton site is a stark reminder of the consequences of the failure to level the playing field for manufacturing. Carter pulls a map from his pocket. On it are a series of red crosses representing other major facilities that have been abandoned.

Among them is the “Olefins 6” ethylene cracker, or at least what remains of it. Permanently shut down last year by its Saudi owners, engineers are already in the process of dismantling a facility that had been a feature of the Teesside skyline since it began operating almost half a century ago.

“This is all decontaminated,” Carter says, nodding to a dizzying maze of metal pipes, tubes and towers. Some of the pipes have been cut, meaning the plant can never be revived.

Olefins 6 used extreme heat to break down, or “crack”, hydrocarbons into ethylene, a raw material used by neighbouring plants.

Experts warn of a dangerous domino effect: when one facility disappears, it imperils the existence of others in close proximity because of the inter-dependency that is a common feature of the chemicals industry.

“The issue with all of this is that the industry is incredibly integrated,” Paul Hodges, a former ICI director-turned-consultant, says.

“Every time you lose one of those adjoining plants, you lose efficiency, and you lose the ability to make certain products. The main issue ... is that if you allow that plant to close, actually, you’re going to close this, this, this and this because of that inter-alliance.”

“Our industry’s biggest customer is itself,” adds Elliott, of the Chemicals Industry Association (CIA).

On the other side of the River Tees sits CF Industries’ Billingham complex, which produces ammonia. If anything captures Britain’s muddled approach to industrial policy, it is surely this.

In 2021 and again in 2022, the American company paused the machinery at Wilton, and another facility in Ince, Cheshire, citing sharp rises in natural gas prices, high carbon costs, and dwindling demand. Cheaper imports were also a factor, it said.

Ministers panicked. Ammonia is a key ingredient in the fertilisers that ensure farmers can keep the supermarket shelves stocked with food. It is also vital to the defence industry as a core component in the manufacture of munitions and explosives.

But it was the threat to carbon dioxide supplies – a byproduct of ammonia production – that prompted Boris Johnson’s government to step in with a bailout thought to be worth tens of millions of pounds to ensure the lights stayed on. CF Fertilisers was responsible for about 60pc of the UK’s commercial CO2 supplies, essential for fizzy drinks and fresh food but nuclear power too.

Yet despite the generosity of taxpayers, the company shut the Ince site permanently, and at Billingham switched to importing ammonia from the US, instead of producing it locally. Ammonia now features on a growing list of hugely important industrial chemicals that Britain obtains entirely from overseas.

Alex Cunningham, the then-Labour MP for Stockton-on-Tees, whose former constituency houses the plant, said the decision was the result of an “abject lack of action and urgency to support energy-intensive industries” on the part of ministers. “Any pretence at an industrial strategy has been abandoned,” he claimed at the time.

According to Sky News, Britain has lost 11 critical factories in the space of a decade.

The closures are part of a wider crunch that has resulted in the disappearance of 25 chemical plants in the last five years alone, the CIA calculates. This rapid retrenchment has left Britain with either no production at all or “limited” domestic output of 11 basic yet vital chemicals including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, and sulphuric acid.

Last month, in a letter to the Prime Minister, the CIA warned that the decline in chemical output reached 60pc last year. The Association says the sector is now facing “the fight of its life”.

‘We need urgent action’

There are tentative signs that the Government is listening.

In December, it stepped in with a £125m support package to help save the last remaining ethylene factory in Grangemouth. A reported £100m to protect CO2 supplies at Ensus in Teesside is viewed among some industry figures as further proof that ministers are beginning to grasp what is at stake.

The Department for Business and Trade said the reopening of the Ensus plant formed part of “wider government work” designed to ensure the UK maintained access to critical industrial resources during global supply shocks.

Meanwhile, a spokesman for the Government said it was working with industry on potential solutions to the Middle East energy crisis.

They said: “We’re moving full steam ahead to tackle this which is why we’ve taken action to cut electricity costs for industries across Great Britain.”

On the other hand, there are plenty of recent examples of critical industrial capacity – not just in chemicals – being allowed to vanish with barely a whimper from Labour officials.

Grangemouth oil refinery, on the Firth of Forth, and Prax Lindsey refinery, on the banks of the Humber estuary, both closed for good last year, knocking out a third of the UK’s refining capacity and leaving households and businesses even more exposed to global price shocks.

The remaining four refineries could soon follow. A cross-party group of MPs has warned that those left standing face the risk of closure because they are included in the UK Emissions Trading Scheme – a carbon tax that ratchets up annually. But as they point out, foreign refineries face no such taxes, enabling them to increasingly undercut rivals here.

The levy “risks further refinery closures and at worst the UK becoming entirely dependent on imported fuel at a time of unprecedented global instability and geopolitical risk”, Elizabeth de Jong, the chief executive of Fuels Industry UK, said.

“This is a political decision and the Treasury is knowingly increasing the competitive disadvantage faced by our remaining refineries. Ministers say they value energy security and the UK’s industrial base, but now we need urgent action,” she added.

Elsewhere, India’s Tata has been cajoled into turning off its blast furnaces in Port Talbot, South Wales, in the name of making the planet greener, with hundreds of millions of pounds of taxpayers’ money. The move cost 2,000 local steelworkers their jobs. Similar plans are afoot at British Steel in Scunthorpe, Lincolnshire, though there it has chosen to protect jobs through nationalisation.

Huntsman says the decisions sum up how muddled industrial and energy policies are in Britain. The idea that “shutting down steel mills” and “moving capacity to India ... is an environmental victory is astounding,” he says.

“You’re destroying the economy, your tax base and your regulatory base,” Huntsman adds.

“You’re shipping these jobs and the CO2 elsewhere to countries that are far worse regulated, so you’re destroying the environment too. Yet people are patting themselves on the back, saying that they’re cleaning up the UK.”

Risk to jobs

Instead of slowing the charge towards net zero in the face of the Middle East crisis, Miliband and the Government have pledged to double down on renewables.

Calls to make Britain less reliant on foreign imports by fast-tracking new drilling licences in major North Sea fields such as Jackdaw and Rosebank have been dismissed. Instead, Labour believes the Iran energy shock is further proof that Britain needs to continue to move away from fossil fuels.

The Government has stepped in with some relief from energy costs. The Energy Intensive Industries scheme – conceived in 2016 – has been updated to include greater relief from some net zero levies for high-energy manufacturers.

The more recent British industrial competitiveness scheme cuts electricity bills for manufacturers by 25pc, but the bar for eligibility is high and applicants must pass a whole series of tests first. Even then, it doesn’t kick in for another year.

Huntsman dismisses such initiatives as “whistling past the graveyard”. “So you’re going to take all these taxes that would have put us out of business and suspend them for the short-term?” he asks.

He points to a recent report from Roland Berger, a management consultancy, that showed an 81pc fall in capital spend among Europe’s chemicals producers.

“If you’ve got a vehicle and you’re suspending 81pc of your maintenance of that vehicle, that means the vehicle is being driven until the wheels literally fall off,” says Huntsman. “They’re putting just enough money in to keep it running until the next major issue comes up and then it’s down for good, and when these things [factories] go down, they stay down.”

In Teesside, one of Carter’s great frustrations is that Huntsman Corporation has spent tens of millions of pounds making its Teesside plant more energy-efficient. “We are as efficient as possible but because of the raw material costs that are feeding through, we can’t remain competitive. It’s not a level playing field,” he says.

Mustapha, a local taxi driver whose grandfather was employed at the nearby British Steel site before it closed in 2015, is concerned about the effect of seemingly endless factory closures on an area he has lived in for 26 years.

Could the thousands of jobs being lost to deindustrialisation be replaced by employment on green projects, as Labour claims?

South Korean steel specialist SeAH opened a wind power components factory down the road last year, but Mustapha is doubtful the sectors of the future can bring the same benefits as the heavy industry of old.

“Those jobs are for the educated few. The steelworks provided jobs for everybody. Look at those on the right,” he says pointing to a row of crumbling red brick buildings on the short journey to Wilton. “All this was built for the British Steel people.”

He remembers a pizza takeaway that used to serve the steelworkers. “They made thousands [of pounds] a night ... but that business is finished.” In Labour’s hands, the same may soon be said of British industry.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Least effective Tube strike for years as union loses its grip
data.description:
The RMT union’s Tube strike has been its least effective walkout for years, according to official data.

More than half of scheduled services operated normally across all London Underground (LU) lines on Tuesday, despite the 24-hour walkout.

This is a significant contrast to the union’s last strike in September, when all lines were suspended.

That Tube services were maintained despite the industrial action suggests that, for the first time in many years, the militant RMT has lost its grip.

Thomas Turrell, the Conservatives’ City Hall transport spokesman, hailed the news as a “bitter disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London”.

“Whilst it is a relief that the full damage of this strike did not come to pass, we must not ignore the fact that these strikes come only because the unions think that our weak Labour Mayor will capitulate to them,” he warned.

Around 60 per cent of Tube drivers turned up for work on Tuesday, an LU source confirmed.

Other sources said they could not remember the last time an RMT strike had resulted in more than half of planned services running.

All 10,000 of the RMT’s LU members walked out in September, in contrast to this week’s strikes where only drivers refused to work.

Many had believed that a Tube-wide RMT drivers’ strike could force most trains to be cancelled.

Yet, just under two-thirds of the usual service level operated on the Bakerloo, Victoria and Northern lines during Tuesday’s strike, LU told The Telegraph.

The Jubilee and Metropolitan lines kept up more than half of their normal timetable, even though the latter was part-suspended between Baker Street and Aldgate.

Londoners generally heeded warnings from Transport for London (TfL) that “significant disruption” was expected, with officials recording a 13 per cent drop in the number of contactless cards tapping in on Tuesday compared with the same day last year.

This equates to around half a million fewer travellers, having decreased from 3.9 million tap-ins last year to 3.4 million earlier this week.

Passenger numbers were also down 8 per cent on the Overground and 13 per cent on the DLR. Neither line is affected by strikes, although many use these services to change to the Tube.

Around 4 per cent more people used the Elizabeth Line than usual, however, reflecting the part-suspension of the Central Line across its middle stretch between White City and Liverpool Street. The Elizabeth Line parallels the Central as it passes through the middle of the capital.

Fewer Tube drivers members of RMT

The second of this week’s 24-hour walkouts will end at 12pm on Friday, with transport bosses warning that Tube trains will not restart until 7.30am at the earliest.

Around half of London’s 3,300 Tube drivers are members of the RMT. This figure used to be significantly higher but many have either given up their union membership or joined the rival Aslef union.

The RMT’s strikes this week were called over what it described as the “imposing” of a four-day working week on Tube drivers. Union bosses say this will result in longer shifts of up to eight hours and 45 minutes.

Aslef secured Tube bosses’ agreement to a 35-hour week, which is being offered to all drivers on a voluntary basis. The RMT, however, wants drivers’ working hours cut to 32 per week with no reduction in pay.

Mr Turrell said: “It will be a disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London that their own shortcomings have prevented them bringing the city to a standstill.

“The Conservatives created legislation to ensure this level of service as a legal minimum during strikes, which Labour promptly dropped – and as such, any strike which is more damaging to London than the one we have seen this week will be a blatant political choice by authorities.”

An RMT spokesman said: “Our strike is solid and reflects our growing presence in the driver grade in London Underground.

“TfL need to focus on revising the offer our members have overwhelmingly rejected to avert further disruption in the coming months.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Barclays suffers £230m hit from shadow bank collapse
data.description:
Barclays has suffered a £228m hit from the collapse of a “shadow bank” at the centre of an alleged fraud.

The FTSE 100 lender revealed the impairment in its latest quarterly results on Tuesday, claiming it was related to a “single name” company, understood to be Market Financial Solutions (MFS).

MFS billed itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and short-term loans. The company was part of a fast-growing crop of so-called bridging lenders in Britain, often charging higher interest rates.

However, the lender was plunged into administration last month amid what a judge called “very serious” allegations of fraud. Paresh Raja, MFS’s founder, has denied the claims.

As a shadow bank, MFS did not take deposits, but instead funded its loans by borrowing from banks and other financial firms.

Shadow banking is a form of private credit, where loans come from non-banking sources such as hedge funds, which are subject to less strict regulations. The sector has grown rapidly since the financial crisis, as tighter regulations on traditional banks pushed riskier forms of finance into unregulated markets.

A cast of high-profile banks, including Barclays, helped bankroll MFS’s expansion, leaving them exposed to hundreds of millions of pounds in potential write-offs following its collapse.

However, despite the hit from MFS and a £105m increase in provisions related to the motor finance mis-selling scandal, Barclays still recorded pre-tax profits of £2.8bn. Shares in Barclays dropped 2.9pc on Tuesday.

The British banking giant has been buoyed by interest rates staying higher for longer as a result of the war in Iran. This helped its net interest income – the difference between the interest rate it receives on bank deposits and charges for loans – increase by 9pc to almost £2bn.

Higher volatility because of the war also boosted Barclays’ investment bank, with revenues in its advisory and equity capital markets division rising by 78pc and 31pc respectively.

In the wake of MFS’s collapse, CS Venkatakrishnan, the chief executive of Barclays, said the bank was constraining lending to similar businesses, including those with vulnerable business models.

“These entities neither represent a material exposure nor a material source of foregone income, but their risk far outweighs any reward,” Mr Venkatakrishnan said.

It is not the first time the bank has taken a financial hit following an alleged fraud relating to shadow banking. It took a £110m hit last year, related to the collapse of Tricolor, a US car financing lender.

The bank is also reducing its presence in private credit more broadly, cutting its exposure to the sort of “direct lending” that has come under pressure in the US.

Mr Venkatakrishnan said some of these “highly leveraged non-investment-grade corporates” could become vulnerable to a weakening economy.

Barclays said that its total exposure to shadow banks was £66bn. The bulk of this was in its securitised products business, which includes its lending to companies such as Tricolor and MFS.

The bank said it had £15bn tied to private credit. It also had £1bn in exposure to business development companies, a popular investment vehicle for private credit which has recently come under scrutiny because of its ties to the tech sector.

It comes as investors have pushed banks to provide more disclosure on their ties to the shadow banking sector. Deutsche Bank revealed last month that it had €26bn (£22.6bn) at stake in private credit.

There have been growing concerns that some private credit firms have lent too heavily to the tech sector.

Several major US-based private credit firms have seen their share prices drop dramatically this year over concerns about their exposure to high-risk technology investments.

data.title: German carmakers sue City watchdog over £9bn car finance scheme
data.description:
Two German carmakers are suing the City watchdog over its £9bn car finance redress scheme.

Mercedes-Benz and Volkswagen have both launched legal challenges against the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) over the scheme, which will compensate customers for mis-sold car loans.

A Volkswagen spokesman said it had “identified issues that require independent clarification” and so had decided to take the FCA to court. “Our referral is focused on ensuring the redress scheme is applied accurately and fairly,” they added.

Analysts have said the carmaker could face a bill of approximately £1.5bn for the scheme.

A spokesman for Mercedes-Benz, which is expected to take a €422m (£366m) hit, confirmed it has challenged the compensation package but would not comment further.

Twelve million motorists are poised to receive an average payout of £829 under the FCA’s scheme, which will cost the car finance industry as much as £9.1bn.

This includes £7.5bn in redress payments to customers and £1.6bn in administrative costs.

This compensation is designed to pay customers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

Credit Agricole, the French bank, is also reportedly considering challenging the scheme, according to Sky News. It declined to comment.

A spokesman for the FCA said that it had received challenges from three lenders. “We are considering our approach and will set out more later this week,” they added.

Businesses ranging from banks to the finance arms of car manufacturers are liable to pay compensation.

It comes after FirstRand, a South African bank which owns roughly a tenth of the UK car finance sector through MotoNovo, vowed to abandon its motor finance business in the UK because of the redress package.

Under the scheme, FirstRand faces a bill of £750m, which it said was “disproportionate and unfair”.

So far, most British banks have publicly declined to challenge the scheme.

Lloyds Bank, which is the UK’s largest provider of vehicle loans, ruled out a challenge several weeks ago. It is facing payouts of roughly £1.95bn.

“While we remain disappointed in and disagree with [the FCA’s] conclusions, we believe that moving forward with the scheme is now the right step for our customers and shareholders,” the bank said in a statement.

Others soon did the same, with both Barclays and Santander confirming they would not appeal.

The Finance and Leasing Association, the trade body that represents most motor finance companies, also decided against mounting a legal challenge.

Separately, the FCA scheme is under pressure from Consumer Voice, a consumer group, which has partnered with Courmacs Legal, a mass-claims law firm, to challenge the scheme in court and increase the amount of compensation.

“The FCA has designed a scheme that leaves ordinary motorists hundreds of pounds per claim out of pocket. That cannot be left unchallenged,” Alex Neill, a co-founder of Consumer Voice, said this month.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: London’s house price plunge is a warning sign for the whole economy
data.description:
Prices in Westminster are down by 12pc over the last year. In Kensington and Chelsea they are down by 11.2pc and by just as much in the City of London.

Over the last few weeks, it has become clear that house prices in the swankiest parts of London are in freefall. Plenty of people will be relaxed about that, and a few might even celebrate it if it means that living in the capital becomes more affordable. There is just one catch. It has the potential to blow up the whole economy – and a housing crash is the last thing Britain needs right now.

Anyone who thought that an apartment, or even better a whole house, in one of the smarter parts of London was the safest possible investment, or indeed a guarantee of lavish retirement, will have had a rude shock if they have checked Rightmove recently.

Prices are collapsing. According to the latest data from the Office for National Statistics, prices in all London’s most affluent boroughs are suffering from double-digit declines, and have now been falling for five consecutive months.

They are still expensive by any normal standards. In Kensington and Chelsea, the average price is still £1.2m, and in Westminster £872,000.

But they have suffered the biggest slump since the 2008-09 financial crash, and are now back to 2013 levels. In effect, a whole decade of gains has been wiped out, and if you were unlucky enough to have bought at anything close to the peak you will now be sitting on a big loss by now.

In fairness, across the rest of the country, there is not going to be a lot of sympathy. Prices in central London were wildly overvalued anyway. Many of the properties were owned by rich foreigners, who hardly even bothered to visit them very often. The speculators can afford the losses. It might even make it easier for first-time buyers to find somewhere to live. If anything, we should be celebrating housing becoming a little more affordable. It will make life easier for everyone.

Perhaps. The trouble is, it could also turn into a huge problem for the wider British economy. There are four big issues.

To start with, it is the most direct confirmation we have that the wealthy are fleeing London. It is the only explanation for the sudden falls: people want to leave and they don’t mind taking a loss on the way out. They might not be going to Dubai any more, but they are going somewhere else instead. Closing the non-dom scheme, raising capital gains taxes and the highest inheritance tax in the developed world have driven them out of the capital.

The class warriors in Labour and the Green Party might welcome that. Bashing the rich is their favourite occupation. But as you lose the wealthiest people, you also lose all the jobs they create and the investment they bring into the country as well. It is hardly good for the economy.

Next, it is going to turn into a huge drain on the Treasury. All those Westminster and Chelsea apartments were paying 12pc in stamp duty and often more if the owners were foreign or if it was a second home. Stamp duty brings in £15bn a year, and on some estimates more than half of that came from the top band on properties above £1.5m.

At a rough estimate, a collapse in the London market would cost Rachel Reeves between £6bn and £7bn a year, and since this Government refuses to control spending, that money would have to be found from higher borrowing or higher taxes on the rest of us. As for the planned mansion tax on properties worth more than £2m, perhaps we should scrap it now. At this rate, there won’t be enough left to make it worth the hassle.

Thirdly, it will trap lots of people in negative equity. Anyone who bought at any time over the last decade, especially if they took out a big mortgage, as most of them probably did, will now be stuck with a property that is worth less than the debt secured on it.

They won’t be able to move even if they can sell their home, because they will have no way of repaying the loan. If they lose their job and can’t keep up repayments, the mortgage lender will have to take the hit. Just as seriously, through the Help To Buy scheme, the Government may well face heavy losses on its own loans, and that will leave the public finances in even worse shape than they already are.

Finally, price drops in central London are often the first signal of wider falls across the country. House prices nationally have held up remarkably well so far despite the steep rise in interest rates, all the extra taxes, and a stagnating economy. But who says that is going to last?

Anyone who is quietly pleased about the losses suffered on a swanky apartment in Chelsea won’t be quite so happy when very ordinary homes in Bristol or Newcastle are witnessing double-digit price declines. A general house price crash always starts somewhere. You have to be very optimistic to think that falling prices in central London won’t be rippling out to the rest of the country by the end of the year.

In reality, a property crash is the last thing the British economy needs right now. The economy is already close to a recession. Inflation is poised to rise following the surge in oil prices in the wake of the war in Iran leaving the Bank of England with no room to cut interest rates.

The Chancellor is spending so wildly that taxes will inevitably have to rise again in the autumn. There are few jobs being created, and investment is evaporating. It is a dismal outlook.

If house prices start to slide, it would crush consumer spending, and pose a threat to the financial system as well. It hasn’t happened yet – but unfortunately the signals from Westminster and Kensington suggest that it could be on the way.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JP Morgan moves staff from Paris back to London in Brexit climbdown
data.description:
Wall Street’s biggest bank has moved some of its European staff back to London as part of a post-Brexit climbdown.

JP Morgan is relocating workers from its Paris office after overestimating the number of staff needed on the Continent, six years after shifting services to the French capital following the UK’s exit from the European Union.

The relocation of staff marks JP Morgan’s latest rethink in its European operations. The bank’s chief executive, Jamie Dimon, has been a vocal critic of Brexit.

Mr Dimon warned in 2021 that the bank may eventually move all of its European operations out of London following the decision to leave the EU.

However, it has increased its total number of UK staff from 18,000 to 23,000 over the last decade after launching its consumer banking arm, Chase, in the UK.

The whole of JP Morgan employs 13,000 staff in London.

By contrast, it has more than 1,000 staff working in Paris, a figure that has quadrupled since Brexit but is still dwarfed by its number of London staff.

It is not clear how many staff are moving from Paris to London.

However, a spokesman for the bank told Bloomberg that it was “committed to our sizeable operations on the Continent for the long term”.

Mr Dimon, the bank’s long-serving chief executive, said in the run-up to the 2016 vote that the investment giant could cut up to 4,000 UK jobs if Leave won.

However, it later scaled back these estimates and said no more than 500 to 1,000 roles would move abroad.

A vocal backer of Remain before the referendum, Mr Dimon said that Brexit “cannot possibly be a positive” for the UK economy in the near term.

In his annual letter to shareholders in 2021, Mr Dimon wrote: “Brexit was accomplished, but many issues still need to be negotiated. And in those negotiations, Europe has had, and will continue to have, the upper hand.

“In the short run (ie the next few years), this cannot possibly be a positive for the UK’s GDP – the effect after that will be completely based upon whether the UK has a comprehensive and well-executed strategic plan that is acceptable to Europe.”

However, JP Morgan is preparing to bolster its presence in London by building the tallest skyscraper in Canary Wharf, which will become its new UK headquarters, housing up to 12,000 staff.

Meanwhile, Paris has become more of a financial hub since the Brexit referendum, with major international banks expanding their presence in France.

Goldman Sachs moved into a new 9,000 sq metres Paris headquarters in 2022, while Barclays announced earlier this year it was moving its European base to the French capital from Dublin.

The Euronext exchange in Paris has taken London’s crown as Europe’s largest stock market, although the City briefly regained the title in 2024.

JP Morgan declined to comment when contacted by The Telegraph.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: London office construction hits 10-year low in blow to Starmer
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s hopes of getting Britain building have been dealt a major setback after office construction in London plunged 35pc to its lowest level in a decade.

Construction work began on around 4.8 million square feet of office space in the capital in 2025, according to Deloitte’s latest London office crane survey. It found the number of new-build office projects more than halved year-on-year to just 1.6 million square feet of space.

Deloitte warned that the slump in building work risked leaving the UK with a shortage of office space as soon as 2027.

Philip Parnell, the head of real estate valuation at Deloitte, said the downturn had been driven by the “complexity involved in navigating the planning system”. He said developers were also struggling to “manage and mitigate” rising build costs.

Just one of the five biggest developments that began last year – a 24-storey office tower in the City of London, known as the Dovetail Building – was a new-build project. The remaining four consisted of refurbishments.

The data follow warnings that building new schemes in London has become especially difficult for property developers.

Mr Parnell said developers were more cautious about whether new projects would be financially viable and whether they could deliver them as they grappled with growing red tape.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise, the chief executive of Next, warned this week that the country’s planning system was the “biggest drag on British prosperity”.

He proposed that the entire rulebook for planning should be rewritten in favour of a market-based approach, adding: “As with some terrible buildings, sometimes we have to tear down and build again because planned economies don’t work.”

The slowdown in office construction in London will come as a blow to the Prime Minister, who came to power promising the “biggest building boom in a generation” through planning reforms. He pledged to “take an axe to red tape”.

However, since then, the number of new homes built has also fallen to a decade-low.

Mr Parnell said: “Developers still see a planning process as complex and expensive and time-consuming.”

He warned the situation risked worsening as builders braced for a rise in construction costs from the war in Iran.

Mr Parnell said: “There is more than just a precautionary note here around build-cost inflation and initially that does present a real risk and challenge to delivery.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why are self-service checkouts so loathsome? They make us feel exploited, researchers find
data.description:
“Unexpected item in the bagging area, an assistant is coming to help you.”

The phrase alone is enough to raise the blood pressure, dredging up memories of fraught supermarket shops.

You find yourself standing at the self-service checkout, basket full, while a queue forms behind you. There’s not a human member of staff in sight and the item just won’t scan.

Now researchers have discovered exactly why interacting with the machines is such a loathsome experience – they make us feel “exploited”.

A study has found shoppers feel that they are performing “unpaid labour”, effectively doing the job of a supermarket worker.

Grappling with poorly functioning machines leaves consumers with a sense of “value extraction”, with customers “compelled to perform tasks traditionally assigned to paid workers, without compensation or meaningful control”, the study found.

The paper, produced by a group of academics in Canada, said shoppers often found the convenience of self-service tills was “outweighed by the demands of complex systems”, with customers struggling to use machines often being left with feelings of “dissatisfaction, frustration and a feeling of being exploited by the system”.

The paper discusses how businesses “benefit by shifting labour responsibility and operational burden on to consumers”, taking on tasks previously assigned to paid employees including scanning, bagging, correcting errors and managing payment.

“Instead of hiring more frontline staff, firms deploy additional self-checkouts, shifting labour to consumers under the illusion of choice and convenience,” the study said.

It added that in some cases, automated systems could also increase the risk of theft.

The chairman of Marks & Spencer told The Telegraph on Thursday that self-checkouts were helping to fuel a rise in shoplifting among “good, honest people” in Britain.

Archie Norman said that self-service checkouts had broken the “human link” between shoppers and retailers, resulting in people simply giving up with malfunctioning machines and walking off with their shopping without paying.

Self-service checkouts have surged in number over the past decade and now exceed manned tills in many supermarkets.

Initially designed for smaller shops of a small basket of items, they now extend to trolley-loads of shopping and stretch down the aisles.

Some brands are going against the trend, however. Booths, the upmarket northern supermarket franchise, opted in 2023 to remove self-service tills from virtually all of its shops to improve customer service.

Mariam Hamam, of Toronto Metropolitan University, who wrote the report, said the study concluded that self-service checkouts were convenient and beneficial for some consumers and were not necessarily good or bad.

“It depends on each shop but our study found that, under certain conditions, especially when there is time pressure, there can be a feeling of it feeling like work,” she said.

“If it does not work for consumers, they feel like they are not benefiting, with the store getting all the value of the exchange and the customer getting nothing in return.”

The study, published in the International Journal of Quality and Service Sciences, simulated automated supermarket checkouts between two groups – one dealing with simple transactions and another dealing with more complex transactions under time pressure. It then measured the emotional responses of those taking part.

It was co-authored by Mathieu Lajante and Dewi Tojib.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain at risk of mobile signal rationing as energy costs soar
data.description:
Mobile network operators have warned they could be forced to ration phone signal access to combat soaring energy prices triggered by the war in Iran.

Telecoms giants including VodafoneThree, Virgin Media O2 and BT-owned EE have issued a stark warning to the Government over energy costs after being excluded from Rachel Reeves’s support scheme.

Some companies are understood to be drawing up contingency plans as they grapple with heightened power costs.

These include rationing access to their networks or slowing down speeds to help reduce energy use. Another option is surge pricing, which would charge customers an additional fee at peak times.

Mobile phone services such as calls and internet data could be the worst affected, although broadband could also be hit.

Bosses warned that rising energy costs could also force the companies to scale back plans to expand their 5G networks, resulting in jobs either being cut or moved offshore.

In response to the threat, a Government spokesman said mobile network operators are required by law to maintain connectivity.

Industry executives are frustrated at Labour’s decision to exclude telecoms companies from a recent support package aimed at shielding businesses from soaring energy costs.

The Chancellor unveiled plans to cut electricity bills for 10,000 manufacturers by up to 25pc under the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS) last week, though the measures will not come into effect until April 2027.

Telecoms companies believe their sector warrants support as an operator of critical national infrastructure.

One source branded it a “serious oversight”, adding that it raised questions about which parts of the economy the Government was prioritising.

Mobile networks are highly energy-intensive, consuming just under 1 terawatt-hour of electricity each year. That is the equivalent of powering 370,000 homes a year.

Operators hedge their energy costs to help weather price spikes.

However, prices have still increased by 70pc in recent years as a result of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the outbreak of war in the Middle East, which led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial trade artery that transports a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies.

The price of electricity is mostly set by gas, which has risen by 33pc since the start of the war in Iran.

Telecoms bosses argue the sector is disproportionately exposed to the crisis due to the “always on” nature of mobile networks, meaning they are unable to shift demand to cheaper, off-peak times.

Any move to ration or slow down access to mobile signals, which is understood to be a worst-case scenario, would be a major blow to Britons already frustrated by patchy coverage. The UK ranks bottom of the G7 for 5G download speeds.

It could also deal a setback to the UK’s sluggish economy, with connectivity estimated to be worth £6.6bn per year.

Ms Reeves is already facing a backlash from other business leaders over her BICS measures, which they say do not go far enough and would arrive too late to prevent job losses.

A spokesman for VodafoneThree said: “We are disappointed that the Government has chosen not to include the telecoms sector in the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme.

“We urge the Government to consider the impact of rising energy prices on the vital telecoms sector that unlocks growth in all parts of the economy.”

BT and Virgin Media O2 said rising energy prices could hinder long-term investment in the UK, but said they had no plans to ration access to their networks.

A Department for Energy spokesman said: “UK communications providers must legally maintain network availability.”
data.author: The telegraph
